checks.a_analysis: The HTML output faithfully follows the visual organization and flow of the source PDF. Content is presented in a single-column scrolling layout which is appropriate for a web conversion of a slide deck. The hierarchical relationship between titles and their corresponding content is maintained throughout the document. Navigation elements like the Table of Contents are added as expected.

The tables on pages 6, 7, and 8 of the source PDF have been successfully converted into functional HTML tables. The table on page 6 ('Post-Matthew Ground Truth Locations') is rendered with clear columns for Longitude, Latitude, Location, and State. The complex table on page 8 ('Volume Change...') is also correctly rendered as a data table with headers and data rows that are easily readable and logically organized. No visual corruption or overlapping text is observed in any of the tables.

Meaningful images from the end of the document are missing in the HTML output. Specifically, the aerial photo with flight paths on page 12, the color-coded relative depth heat map on page 13, and the bathymetric nautical chart on page 14 are all absent. While the headings for these sections appear in the HTML, no corresponding image content follows them. Most other meaningful images (maps and screenshots from pages 4-11) are present.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF contains significant imagery on pages 12 (Aerial photo with flight paths), 13 (Relative Depth Image heat map), and 14 (Nautical chart overlay).
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML output contains the headings for these pages ('Hatteras Inlet Satellite-Derived Bathy' and 'Hurricane Matthew Request - Landsat') but no images or alternative visual content is rendered beneath them.
checks.b_evidence_location: End of document, sections corresponding to pages 12, 13, and 14.

checks.a_analysis: There are no instances of duplicated body content (paragraphs, tables, or images) in the HTML output. Titles appearing in the navigation menu/Table of Contents and then again in the main content area is an expected accessibility feature and not a defect.
